enrichment.short_description: BorgBackup is a free, open-source deduplicating backup program for Linux, macOS, and BSD, combining space-efficient compression with authenticated 256-bit encryption.
enrichment.seo_title: BorgBackup: Free Open-Source Deduplicating Backup Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BorgBackup is a free, open-source command-line backup tool with content-defined deduplication, compression, and authenticated encryption for Linux, macOS, and BSD.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BorgBackup (commonly called Borg) is a free, open-source deduplicating backup program released under the BSD 3-clause license. It was created in May 2015 when a group of contributors known as "The Borg Collective" forked Attic, an earlier deduplicating backup tool that began development in 2010, because Attic's original maintainer was slow to merge community contributions. Borg 1.0.0 was released on March 5, 2016, and the project has been actively maintained since, with a Borg 2.0 series in development as of 2026. Borg has no company behind it and no headquarters; it is a community-maintained open-source project hosted on GitHub, led by lead developer Thomas Waldmann. BorgBackup's core technical feature is content-defined chunking deduplication: each file is split into variable-length chunks, and only chunks not already present in the repository are stored, which makes daily incremental backups extremely space-efficient even across many similar files or snapshots. On top of deduplication, Borg applies client-side compression (choices include lz4, zstd, zlib, or lzma, or no compression) and authenticated 256-bit encryption using AES-OCB or ChaCha20-Poly1305, so data can be safely stored on untrusted or remote servers such as SSH-accessible storage, rsync.net, or BorgBase. Because encryption and chunking happen client-side, the backup destination never sees unencrypted data. Other notable features include the ability to mount a Borg archive as a regular filesystem using FUSE, letting users browse and restore individual files from a backup without extracting the entire archive; flexible pruning and retention policies for rotating old backups; and archive integrity verification. Borg runs natively on Linux, macOS, and FreeBSD/other BSD variants; it does not have official native Windows support, though it can be run inside WSL on Windows. There is no graphical installer or web dashboard bundled with core Borg — it is operated entirely from the command line, though third-party wrappers such as Borgmatic and Vorta provide scheduling and GUI layers on top of it. Because BorgBackup is free software with no paid tiers, there is no vendor pricing to evaluate; the only costs a user incurs are for whatever storage backend they choose to push encrypted archives to (a home server, NAS, cloud VM, or a Borg-compatible hosted service like BorgBase or rsync.net). BorgBackup is best suited to technically comfortable users — system administrators, DevOps engineers, and self-hosting enthusiasts — who want a scriptable, storage-efficient, and cryptographically sound backup solution for servers, workstations, or home labs, rather than end users looking for a polished consumer backup app with a graphical interface out of the box.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: BorgBackup is a free, open-source deduplicating archiver for Linux, macOS, and BSD systems. It was forked from the Attic project in May 2015 by a group of contributors seeking faster, more open development, and reached its 1.0 release in March 2016.
Borg has no company or commercial entity behind it — it is maintained by an open-source community under the BSD 3-clause license, distributed via GitHub, package managers, and PyPI.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Borg's defining feature is content-defined chunking deduplication, which splits files into variable-length chunks and stores each unique chunk only once across all backups in a repository. This makes repeated daily backups of large, mostly-unchanged data sets remarkably storage-efficient.

All data is encrypted and authenticated client-side using 256-bit AES-OCB or ChaCha20-Poly1305 before it ever leaves the source machine, so backup destinations never see unencrypted data. Borg also supports several compression algorithms (lz4, zstd, zlib, lzma) and lets users mount any archive as a browsable filesystem via FUSE.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: BorgBackup itself is entirely free and open source with no paid tiers, license fees, or subscriptions. The only cost involved is whatever storage destination a user chooses — a local disk, NAS, self-managed server, or a Borg-compatible hosting service such as BorgBase or rsync.net.

features.title: Deduplication
features.description: Content-defined chunking ensures only new or changed data is stored, dramatically shrinking the size of repeated backups.

features.title: Authenticated Encryption
features.description: Client-side 256-bit AES-OCB or ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption protects confidentiality and integrity, even on untrusted storage.

features.title: Compression
features.description: Choose from lz4, zstd, zlib, lzma, or no compression to balance speed against storage savings.

features.title: Mountable Archives
features.description: Any archive can be mounted as a regular filesystem via FUSE for browsing and selective file restore.

features.title: Pruning & Retention
features.description: Built-in retention policies let you keep hourly, daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly backups while discarding the rest.

features.title: Remote & SSH Backends
features.description: Backs up over SSH to any remote server or Borg-compatible host, including rsync.net and BorgBase.

features.title: Archive Integrity Checks
features.description: Repository and archive consistency checks help detect corruption before it becomes a data-loss problem.

pros: Free and fully open source with no licensing cost
Highly space-efficient thanks to content-defined deduplication
Strong, well-audited client-side encryption
Mature, stable, and actively maintained since 2015
Works over plain SSH with almost any remote storage
cons: No official native Windows support (WSL only)
Command-line only — no bundled graphical interface
No managed cloud dashboard or centralized fleet management
Steeper learning curve for non-technical users

faqs.question: Is BorgBackup free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. BorgBackup is free, open-source software licensed under BSD 3-clause with no paid tiers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: Does BorgBackup run on Windows?
faqs.answer: Not natively. Borg officially supports Linux, macOS, and BSD; Windows users typically run it inside WSL.

faqs.question: How is BorgBackup different from Attic?
faqs.answer: Borg was forked from Attic in 2015 to enable faster, more open community development, and has since diverged with additional features and active maintenance, while Attic development stalled.

faqs.question: Does BorgBackup need a special server?
faqs.answer: No. It works over standard SSH to any remote host with Borg installed, or to Borg-compatible hosting providers like BorgBase and rsync.net.

faqs.question: Is there a graphical interface for BorgBackup?
faqs.answer: Core Borg is command-line only, but third-party front ends such as Vorta (macOS/Linux) and Borgmatic (scheduling/config) add GUI or higher-level tooling on top of it.

use_cases.title: Server & infrastructure backups
use_cases.description: System administrators use Borg to run scheduled, deduplicated, encrypted backups of Linux servers to remote storage.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted home lab backups
use_cases.description: Self-hosters back up NAS devices, home servers, and personal machines to another disk or a remote Borg-compatible host.

use_cases.title: Encrypted offsite archiving
use_cases.description: Individuals and small teams push encrypted, space-efficient archives to third-party storage without trusting the storage provider with plaintext data.

enrichment.short_description: Botify is an enterprise SEO and AI search optimization platform that helps large websites improve technical SEO, content performance, and visibility in search and answer engines.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing (enterprise contracts typically start around $75,000/year)
enrichment.seo_title: Botify: Enterprise SEO & AI Search Optimization Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Botify is an enterprise SEO platform combining technical SEO, log file analysis, and AI/answer-engine optimization, used by Expedia, Macy's, and other large sites.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Botify is an enterprise SEO and AI search optimization platform founded in 2012 by Adrien Menard, Thomas Grange, and Stan Chauvin in Paris, France. The company has since expanded internationally and now operates a dual headquarters model with offices in New York (3 World Trade Center) and Paris, plus additional locations in London, Singapore, Sydney, and Tokyo. Botify employs roughly 200-300 people. The platform is built for organizations managing very large websites — typically e-commerce retailers, publishers, travel companies, and marketplaces with thousands to millions of URLs — where organic search traffic is a material revenue driver and manual SEO auditing is not feasible at scale. Botify's core technical capability is large-scale site crawling combined with server log file analysis: it ingests raw web server logs to show exactly how Googlebot and other crawlers actually traverse a site, which pages get crawled versus ignored, and where crawl budget is wasted, then correlates that with actual organic search performance data pulled from sources like Google Search Console. This log-file-driven approach differentiates Botify from many SEO tools that rely solely on simulated or third-party crawl data. Beyond technical SEO, Botify has expanded into content performance analysis and, more recently, AI search / answer engine optimization — helping brands understand and improve their visibility in AI-generated answers from tools like Google's AI Overviews, ChatGPT, and other LLM-based search experiences, reflecting the platform's current positioning as an "agentic AI search platform." Botify integrates with analytics and marketing stacks including Google Search Console, Adobe Analytics, and BigQuery, and offers APIs and data exports so enterprise teams can pull SEO and crawl data into their own BI tooling. Botify does not publish self-serve pricing; contracts are negotiated directly with its sales team and are typically customized based on the number of URLs crawled, volume of log files processed, and which modules (technical SEO, content, AI search) a customer licenses. Third-party pricing research estimates enterprise contracts commonly range from roughly $75,000 to $400,000+ per year, though official figures are not public. Botify reports serving more than 500 enterprise customers, including recognizable brands such as Expedia, L'Oréal, The New York Times, and Macy's. Because of its scale-oriented crawling infrastructure, log-file analysis depth, and enterprise sales model, Botify is generally not a fit for small businesses or sites with limited page counts; it is purpose-built for large organizations where incremental organic search gains translate into significant revenue, and where dedicated SEO or growth teams need granular, log-verified crawl and indexation data rather than sampled or estimated crawl insights.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Botify is an enterprise SEO platform founded in 2012 in Paris by Adrien Menard, Thomas Grange, and Stan Chauvin. It now maintains offices in New York and Paris along with a presence in London, Singapore, Sydney, and Tokyo, and employs roughly 200-300 people.

The platform is designed for large, complex websites — e-commerce, publishing, travel, and marketplace businesses with thousands to millions of pages — where organic search is a significant revenue channel and manual SEO auditing doesn't scale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Botify's core differentiator is server log file analysis combined with large-scale crawling, giving teams a real, verified picture of how search engine bots actually crawl a site rather than a simulated estimate.

The platform has expanded beyond technical SEO into content performance analytics and AI/answer-engine optimization, helping brands track and improve visibility in AI-generated search answers alongside traditional organic rankings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Botify does not publish list pricing. Contracts are negotiated directly with the sales team and scaled to the number of URLs crawled, log file volume, and which product modules are licensed. Industry estimates place typical enterprise contracts between roughly $75,000 and $400,000+ per year.

features.title: Log File Analysis
features.description: Ingests raw server logs to show exactly how search engine crawlers traverse a site and where crawl budget is being wasted.

features.title: Large-Scale Site Crawler
features.description: Crawls websites with millions of URLs to surface indexation, technical, and content issues at enterprise scale.

features.title: Content Performance Analysis
features.description: Correlates content attributes with organic search performance to identify what drives (or hurts) rankings.

features.title: AI Search Optimization
features.description: Tracks and helps improve visibility in AI-generated answers from AI Overviews, ChatGPT, and other LLM-based search experiences.

features.title: Analytics Integrations
features.description: Connects with Google Search Console, Adobe Analytics, and BigQuery to unify SEO data with broader marketing analytics.

features.title: APIs & Data Exports
features.description: Provides APIs and exports so enterprise teams can pipe crawl and SEO data into internal BI systems.

pros: Log-file-verified crawl data, not just simulated crawls
Built to handle sites with millions of URLs
Trusted by major enterprise brands (Expedia, Macy's, L'Oréal, The New York Times)
Expanding AI search/answer engine visibility tracking
cons: No public self-serve pricing; enterprise sales cycle required
Expensive relative to SMB-focused SEO tools
Overkill for small or mid-sized websites
Requires log file access/technical setup for full value

faqs.question: Who is Botify built for?
faqs.answer: Large enterprises with high page-count websites — e-commerce, publishing, travel, and marketplace companies — where organic search is a major revenue channel.

faqs.question: Does Botify have public pricing?
faqs.answer: No. Pricing is custom and negotiated based on URL volume, log data, and modules used; third-party estimates put contracts between roughly $75,000 and $400,000+ per year.

faqs.question: What makes Botify different from other SEO platforms?
faqs.answer: Its core analysis is grounded in real server log file data showing actual crawler behavior, rather than relying solely on simulated crawls.

faqs.question: Does Botify support AI search optimization?
faqs.answer: Yes. Botify has expanded its platform to track and optimize visibility in AI-generated search answers alongside traditional organic search.

faqs.question: Where is Botify based?
faqs.answer: Botify was founded in Paris in 2012 and now operates with headquarters presence in both New York and Paris, plus offices in London, Singapore, Sydney, and Tokyo.

use_cases.title: Enterprise e-commerce SEO
use_cases.description: Large online retailers use Botify's crawling and log analysis to fix indexation and crawl-budget issues across huge product catalogs.

use_cases.title: Publisher content & crawl optimization
use_cases.description: Media and publishing companies use Botify to ensure new articles get crawled and indexed quickly and to analyze which content drives organic traffic.

use_cases.title: AI search visibility tracking
use_cases.description: Brands monitor and optimize their presence in AI-generated answer engines alongside classic organic rankings.

enrichment.short_description: BotWave is a free Telegram bot with 200+ commands covering group moderation, AI chat, games, crypto price tracking, and media downloading.
enrichment.seo_title: BotWave: Free Telegram Bot for Moderation, AI & Games
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BotWave is a free Telegram bot offering 200+ commands for group moderation, AI assistance, games, crypto tracking, and media downloads, with no signup required.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BotWave is a free Telegram bot aimed at group administrators and communities who want moderation, AI assistance, entertainment, and utility features without paying for or coding a custom bot. The bot is added directly to a Telegram chat (via the handle @robotforge_bot, per its official site) and is operated entirely at no cost, with the platform explicitly marketing itself as "100% free" and "free forever" with no signup, credit card, or setup required. According to its official website, BotWave provides more than 200 commands spread across 57 modules and states support for 15+ languages. Its feature set spans several categories: moderation and anti-spam tools such as banning, kicking, muting, anti-raid protection, captcha verification, and flood control; an AI assistant capable of answering questions, translating text, correcting grammar, and generating images; casual games including trivia, hangman, wordle, and other mini-games; live cryptocurrency price tracking with charts and market data; a media downloader that can pull content from platforms such as TikTok, Instagram, Twitter/X, and Reddit; a trust-scoring system that rates users from 0 to 100 based on observed behavior; karma and leveling systems with leaderboards; and tools for enforcing mandatory channel subscriptions before users can participate in a group. Users can connect to the bot via QR scan, bot token, or userbot credentials from a single dashboard, according to platform marketing. BotWave's official site explicitly states it is "not affiliated with Telegram or WhatsApp," and despite some third-party listings describing it loosely as a WhatsApp-and-Telegram bot platform, the bot's own site confirms its functionality is delivered exclusively through Telegram, with a page comparing itself against WhatsApp rather than supporting it. No founder, parent company, incorporation details, or headquarters location are publicly disclosed on the official BotWave site, and there is no verifiable information about the team or company behind the project; support is offered informally through a Telegram channel (@forgebotwave) and chat group (@botwaveforgechat) rather than through conventional enterprise support channels. Because BotWave is entirely free with no paid tiers, there are no pricing plans, subscription levels, or upsells to evaluate — its value proposition is a broad, no-cost command library for Telegram group management rather than a commercial SaaS offering. It is best suited to Telegram group and channel administrators — including crypto communities, hobbyist groups, and casual chat communities — who want an all-in-one moderation and entertainment bot without vetting or paying for multiple single-purpose bots, though prospective users should weigh the lack of transparency around who operates the service before granting it administrative permissions in sensitive or large communities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: BotWave is a free, all-in-one Telegram bot that combines group moderation, an AI assistant, casual games, cryptocurrency tracking, and media downloading into a single bot that any Telegram group or channel admin can add.

The platform markets itself as free forever with no signup or credit card required, and its official site states it is not affiliated with Telegram or WhatsApp despite some third-party listings describing it more broadly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: BotWave offers 200+ commands across 57 modules, spanning anti-spam and moderation (banning, muting, anti-raid, captcha, flood control), an AI assistant for Q&A, translation, and image generation, games like trivia and wordle, live crypto price data, and a media downloader for platforms like TikTok, Instagram, and Reddit.

Community features include a 0-100 trust-scoring system, karma and leveling with leaderboards, and forced-subscription enforcement for linked channels, aimed at helping admins manage larger, more active Telegram communities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: BotWave is entirely free, with the official site stating it is free forever and requires no setup, signup, or credit card. There are no paid tiers or premium upsells published on the platform.

features.title: Moderation & Anti-Spam
features.description: Ban, kick, mute, anti-raid protection, captcha verification, and flood control to keep Telegram groups under control.

features.title: AI Assistant
features.description: Answers questions, translates text, corrects grammar, and generates images directly inside a Telegram chat.

features.title: Games
features.description: Built-in casual games including trivia, hangman, wordle, and other mini-games for group entertainment.

features.title: Crypto Price Tracking
features.description: Live cryptocurrency prices, charts, and market data on demand.

features.title: Media Downloader
features.description: Downloads media from platforms such as TikTok, Instagram, Twitter/X, and Reddit directly via bot commands.

features.title: Trust Scoring & Karma
features.description: Rates users 0-100 on behavior and tracks karma/leveling with leaderboards to surface active, trustworthy members.

pros: Completely free with no signup or credit card required
Large command library (200+ commands, 57 modules) covering multiple use cases
Simple setup via QR scan, bot token, or userbot credentials
Claims support for 15+ languages
cons: Telegram only, despite some third-party listings implying WhatsApp support
No public information about the operating company, founder, or headquarters
Support is limited to informal Telegram channels rather than conventional customer support
No paid/enterprise tier for guaranteed SLAs or dedicated support

faqs.question: Is BotWave really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. BotWave's official site states it is free forever with no signup, credit card, or setup fees.

faqs.question: Does BotWave work on WhatsApp?
faqs.answer: No. Despite some third-party descriptions, BotWave's own site confirms it operates exclusively on Telegram and states it is not affiliated with WhatsApp.

faqs.question: How many commands does BotWave have?
faqs.answer: BotWave lists more than 200 commands organized into 57 modules covering moderation, AI, games, crypto tracking, and media downloads.

faqs.question: Who operates BotWave?
faqs.answer: No founder, company, or headquarters information is publicly disclosed; support is handled informally via a Telegram channel and chat group.

faqs.question: How do I add BotWave to my Telegram group?
faqs.answer: The bot can be added directly via its Telegram handle and connected via QR scan, bot token, or userbot credentials from its dashboard.

use_cases.title: Telegram group moderation
use_cases.description: Admins use BotWave's anti-spam, anti-raid, and captcha tools to keep large or fast-growing Telegram groups under control.

use_cases.title: Crypto community management
use_cases.description: Crypto-focused Telegram groups use BotWave's live price tracking alongside moderation tools to serve traders and holders.

use_cases.title: Casual community engagement
use_cases.description: Hobbyist and social groups use BotWave's games, karma, and leveling features to keep members engaged.
use_cases.audience: Community managers and casual group admins

enrichment.short_description: Boundary is HashiCorp's open-source, identity-based secure remote access tool that grants least-privilege access to hosts and services without exposing the network.
enrichment.seo_title: HashiCorp Boundary: Identity-Based Secure Remote Access
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Boundary is HashiCorp's open-source tool for identity-based, zero-trust secure remote access to dynamic hosts and services, with a managed HCP option.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Boundary is an open-source secure remote access tool created by HashiCorp and first announced in October 2020. It provides identity-based access to dynamic hosts, services, and applications without requiring users to be granted direct network access (such as a traditional VPN) or to manage and distribute SSH keys and credentials manually. Instead of trusting a user based on their network location, Boundary authenticates and authorizes users centrally, then brokers just-in-time connections to targets — such as SSH servers, RDP hosts, databases, or internal web applications — based on identity and assigned roles, aligning with zero-trust security principles. HashiCorp, the company behind Boundary, was founded in 2012 by Mitchell Hashimoto and Armon Dadgar and is headquartered in San Francisco, California; as of 2026 the company has roughly 2,180 employees and became a wholly owned subsidiary of IBM after IBM's acquisition closed in February 2025 for $6.4 billion, though HashiCorp's product lines, including Boundary, continue to operate under the HashiCorp brand. Boundary is distributed in three main forms: a free, self-managed Community Edition released as open source software that provides core secure access controls, session management, and CLI/API/Terraform-based automation; a commercial Boundary Enterprise for self-managed enterprise deployments with additional governance and support; and HCP Boundary, a HashiCorp-managed cloud offering (generally available since November 2022) available in Standard and Plus tiers that removes the operational burden of running the control plane. Key technical features include dynamic host catalogs that automatically discover and keep an inventory of cloud and on-premises resources current, session visibility and recording for auditing connections after the fact, fine-grained role-based access policies, and integration with HashiCorp Vault for dynamic, short-lived credentials so users never see or store static secrets. Access can be initiated through the Boundary CLI, desktop client, REST API, Terraform provider, or SDKs, making it scriptable for DevOps and platform engineering workflows. HCP Boundary is billed on a usage basis, metered by "HCP Boundary Users" — each unique human or machine identity that takes an authenticated action within a given month — with the per-user rate varying by the Standard or Plus edition chosen; HashiCorp does not publish exact per-user dollar rates on its public pricing page, so buyers must engage with sales or the HCP portal for a quote. Boundary is aimed at DevOps, platform engineering, and security teams that need to grant developers, operators, or automated systems access to dynamic, frequently-changing infrastructure (cloud VMs, containers, databases) without exposing the underlying network, and who are often already using other HashiCorp tools like Vault, Terraform, or Consul as part of their infrastructure stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Boundary is HashiCorp's open-source, identity-based secure remote access solution, first announced in October 2020. It lets organizations grant users and machines access to dynamic hosts and services based on verified identity and role rather than network location, avoiding traditional VPN-style network exposure.

HashiCorp, founded in 2012 and headquartered in San Francisco, became a subsidiary of IBM in February 2025 following a $6.4 billion acquisition, while continuing to develop and support Boundary and its other infrastructure tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Boundary brokers just-in-time connections to SSH, RDP, database, and web targets based on identity and role, with dynamic host catalogs that automatically keep resource inventories current as cloud infrastructure changes.

Session recording and visibility support auditing, while integration with HashiCorp Vault enables dynamic, short-lived credentials so end users never handle static secrets. Access can be automated via CLI, API, Terraform provider, or SDKs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Boundary Community Edition is free and open source for self-managed deployments. HCP Boundary, the managed cloud offering, is available in Standard and Plus tiers and billed on a usage basis per authenticated "HCP Boundary User" per month; exact unit rates are not published and require contacting HashiCorp. A self-managed Boundary Enterprise edition is also available for organizations that want additional features and support without using the managed cloud control plane.

features.title: Identity-Based Access
features.description: Grants access based on authenticated identity and role rather than network location, removing the need for direct VPN access.

features.title: Dynamic Host Catalogs
features.description: Automatically discovers and maintains an up-to-date inventory of cloud and on-premises hosts and services.

features.title: Session Recording & Visibility
features.description: Records and monitors sessions for auditing and compliance review after the fact.

features.title: Vault Integration
features.description: Issues dynamic, short-lived credentials via HashiCorp Vault so users never see or store static secrets.

features.title: Terraform & API Automation
features.description: Fully automatable via the Boundary Terraform provider, REST API, CLI, and SDKs.

features.title: Multi-Protocol Targets
features.description: Supports SSH, RDP, TCP, and database targets, brokering connections without exposing the underlying network.

pros: Free, open-source Community Edition for self-managed use
Removes need for traditional VPN network exposure
Deep integration with Vault, Terraform, and the wider HashiCorp ecosystem
Session recording supports compliance and audit requirements
cons: Operational complexity to self-host and integrate with existing infrastructure
Advanced features (recording, HA, enterprise governance) require paid Enterprise or HCP tiers
HCP Boundary per-user pricing is not publicly listed
Best value requires familiarity with the broader HashiCorp toolchain

faqs.question: Is HashiCorp Boundary free?
faqs.answer: The self-managed Community Edition is free and open source. Boundary Enterprise and the managed HCP Boundary service are paid offerings with additional features and support.

faqs.question: How is Boundary different from a VPN?
faqs.answer: Boundary grants access based on authenticated identity and role rather than placing a user on the network, so users never get broad network-level access to systems they aren't authorized for.

faqs.question: Does Boundary require other HashiCorp products?
faqs.answer: No, but it integrates closely with Vault for dynamic credentials and Terraform for infrastructure-as-code automation, and many users already run those tools alongside it.

faqs.question: Who owns HashiCorp now?
faqs.answer: HashiCorp became a wholly owned subsidiary of IBM after the acquisition closed in February 2025, though it continues to operate and develop its product line, including Boundary, under the HashiCorp brand.

faqs.question: What protocols does Boundary support for target access?
faqs.answer: Boundary supports SSH, RDP, TCP, and database connections, brokering sessions to these target types based on user identity and role.

use_cases.title: Zero-trust infrastructure access
use_cases.description: Platform engineering teams replace VPN-based access with identity-based, least-privilege connections to cloud infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Auditable privileged access
use_cases.description: Security teams use session recording and role-based policies to grant and audit access to sensitive systems and databases.

use_cases.title: Dynamic cloud resource access
use_cases.description: Organizations with frequently changing cloud infrastructure use dynamic host catalogs to keep access policies current automatically.

enrichment.short_description: Boxy SVG is a lightweight vector graphics editor for creating and editing SVG files, available free in-browser and as a paid desktop app for Mac, Windows, and Linux.
enrichment.seo_title: Boxy SVG: Vector & SVG Editor for Mac, Windows, and Web
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Boxy SVG is a lightweight vector graphics editor for creating and editing SVG files, with on-canvas tools, a code view, and apps for Mac, Windows, Linux, and web.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Boxy SVG is a vector graphics editor built specifically around the SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) file format, created by independent developer Jarek Foksa, who is based in Warsaw, Poland. The first version shipped on the Mac App Store on March 15, 2013, originally written in Objective-C and CoffeeScript. A complete rewrite followed on August 1, 2014, moving the codebase to JavaScript and Electron so the same application could run both as a web app in the browser and as a native desktop app, and a further UI overhaul shipped June 6, 2017 based on Xel, Foksa's own HTML5 widget toolkit. Because it is built and maintained by a solo developer rather than a company, Boxy SVG has no formal headquarters, corporate structure, or public employee count in the conventional sense; its interface is inspired by design tools like Inkscape, Sketch, and Adobe Illustrator. The editor's Chromium-based rendering engine gives it an SVG and CSS code inspector similar to Chrome DevTools, and the app preserves clean SVG output — including metadata like element IDs, classes, and titles — rather than bloating exported files with editor-specific cruft, a common complaint about some competing vector tools. Core editing capabilities include on-canvas manipulation of object geometry, transforms, paint, and other SVG properties; a built-in code view for editing raw SVG markup alongside the visual canvas; path Boolean operations (unite, intersect, subtract, exclude) and other path tools; manual guides, smart guides, and a configurable grid; over 100 configurable keyboard shortcuts; integration with Pixabay for access to over a million free stock photos and vector assets; and Google Fonts integration for typography. Export formats include SVG, SVGZ (compressed SVG), PNG, JPG, WebP, PDF, and HTML5. Boxy SVG runs as a free web app directly in the browser, covering basic SVG editing (shapes, paths, text, gradients, groups) at no cost, and is also distributed as a native desktop app for macOS (via the Mac App Store, priced at $19.99 as a one-time purchase, confirmed on the App Store listing) and for Windows (via the Microsoft Store) and Linux/Chrome OS. Boxy SVG has also introduced a premium web subscription tier — reported by third-party sources at roughly $9.99 per year or per month depending on the plan — that unlocks additional capabilities such as advanced CSS styling and JavaScript editing, along with additional export formats, though the exact current pricing structure for the web premium tiers is best confirmed directly on boxy-svg.com since third-party sources report some inconsistency in the figures. Boxy SVG is best suited to web designers, front-end developers, and hobbyists who need a focused, lightweight SVG editor with direct code-level control, rather than a full-featured illustration or raster-editing suite like Adobe Illustrator or Affinity Designer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Boxy SVG is a vector graphics editor purpose-built around the SVG format, created by independent developer Jarek Foksa. It first launched on the Mac App Store in March 2013 and was rewritten in JavaScript/Electron in 2014 so it could run as both a web app and a native desktop app.

As a solo-developer project rather than a company product, Boxy SVG has no formal headquarters or corporate structure, but it has been continuously updated for over a decade and is distributed on the Mac App Store, Microsoft Store, and the web.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The editor combines on-canvas visual editing with a built-in raw SVG code view, plus a Chromium-based inspector for SVG and CSS similar to Chrome DevTools, letting designers and developers work at both the visual and markup level in the same tool.

Additional capabilities include path Boolean operations, Pixabay and Google Fonts integration, over 100 configurable keyboard shortcuts, and export to SVG, SVGZ, PNG, JPG, WebP, PDF, and HTML5, all aimed at keeping exported SVG markup clean and metadata-preserving.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Boxy SVG's web app is free for basic SVG editing. The Mac desktop app is a one-time $19.99 purchase on the Mac App Store. A premium web subscription tier also exists, reported by third-party sources at roughly $9.99 per year or month depending on plan, unlocking advanced CSS/JavaScript editing and additional export options.

features.title: On-Canvas Vector Editing
features.description: Direct manipulation of object geometry, transforms, and paint properties on a visual canvas.

features.title: Built-in Code View
features.description: Edit raw SVG markup directly alongside the visual canvas for precise, code-level control.

features.title: Path Boolean Operations
features.description: Unite, intersect, subtract, and exclude paths along with other standard path tools.

features.title: Pixabay & Google Fonts Integration
features.description: Access over a million free stock photos/vectors and hundreds of Google Fonts without leaving the app.

features.title: Chromium-Based Inspector
features.description: An SVG and CSS code inspector similar to Chrome DevTools for debugging and fine-tuning output.

features.title: Multi-Format Export
features.description: Export to SVG, SVGZ, PNG, JPG, WebP, PDF, and HTML5.

pros: Lightweight, focused interface compared to full illustration suites
Low one-time cost for the desktop app versus subscription design tools
Works both in-browser and as an offline native app
Preserves clean, metadata-rich SVG output
cons: Far fewer features than Adobe Illustrator or Affinity Designer
Vector/SVG-focused only, with no raster/photo editing
Built and maintained by a solo developer, with no dedicated support team
Some pricing details for premium web tiers are inconsistently reported

faqs.question: Is Boxy SVG free?
faqs.answer: The web app is free for basic SVG editing (shapes, paths, text, gradients, groups). The desktop Mac app is a $19.99 one-time purchase, and a premium web subscription tier also exists.

faqs.question: What platforms does Boxy SVG support?
faqs.answer: Boxy SVG runs as a web app in any modern browser and as a native desktop app for macOS (Mac App Store), Windows (Microsoft Store), Linux, and Chrome OS.

faqs.question: Who makes Boxy SVG?
faqs.answer: Boxy SVG is created and maintained by independent developer Jarek Foksa, based in Warsaw, Poland, rather than a company.

faqs.question: Can I edit raw SVG code in Boxy SVG?
faqs.answer: Yes. Boxy SVG includes a built-in code view for editing SVG markup directly alongside the visual canvas.

faqs.question: How is Boxy SVG different from Adobe Illustrator?
faqs.answer: Boxy SVG is a lighter-weight, SVG-focused editor with a lower one-time cost, while Illustrator is a full-featured, subscription-based illustration suite with far broader capabilities.

use_cases.title: Web icon and UI asset creation
use_cases.description: Web designers and front-end developers create and fine-tune SVG icons, logos, and UI assets with direct code-level control.

use_cases.title: Lightweight illustration
use_cases.description: Hobbyists and indie designers use Boxy SVG as an affordable alternative to subscription-based illustration tools.

use_cases.title: Cross-platform SVG editing
use_cases.description: Users who need to edit SVGs across Mac, Windows, Linux, and the browser use Boxy SVG's consistent cross-platform app.

features: Shapes, paths, text, gradients, and groups

enrichment.short_description: Braintree, a PayPal service, is a payment gateway that lets businesses accept cards, PayPal, Venmo, and digital wallets through a single API integration.
enrichment.seo_title: Braintree: PayPal's Payment Gateway & Merchant API
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Braintree, owned by PayPal, is a payment processing platform offering one API for cards, PayPal, Venmo, Apple Pay, and Google Pay, processing $50B+ annually.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Braintree is a payment processing platform founded by Bryan Johnson in 2007 and headquartered in Chicago, Illinois. PayPal acquired Braintree in 2013 for approximately $800 million, and Braintree has operated as a PayPal service ever since, giving it access to PayPal's broader payments infrastructure, risk management systems, and global reach while continuing to operate its own developer-focused brand and product line. Braintree's core value proposition is a single API integration that lets merchants accept a wide range of payment methods — credit and debit cards, PayPal, Venmo, Apple Pay, Google Pay, and other digital wallets — without having to build and maintain separate integrations for each. This makes it popular with software companies, marketplaces, and platforms that want to offer flexible checkout options to their end customers without the engineering overhead of integrating each payment method individually. The platform provides native SDKs for iOS and Android as well as JavaScript SDKs for web, which supports both custom checkout UIs and Braintree's pre-built Drop-in UI for faster implementation. Additional features include recurring billing and subscription management, a Vault for securely storing customer payment methods for future use (supporting card-on-file and tokenized transactions), and fraud and risk management tools built on PayPal's underlying fraud detection infrastructure. Braintree's standard published pricing for U.S. merchants is 2.9% of the transaction amount plus $0.30 per transaction, a rate broadly comparable to other major payment processors like Stripe; established or high-volume businesses can negotiate custom flat-rate or interchange-plus pricing instead of the standard blended rate. Additional standard fees include a $15 chargeback fee per disputed transaction, along with adjustments for international transactions, non-profit/charitable organizations, and certain other transaction types. Braintree reports processing more than $50 billion in payment volume annually and over one billion transactions per quarter, reflecting its use by a large base of e-commerce and platform businesses. Merchants must go through an approval and underwriting process before they can process live transactions, which is standard for full-service payment gateways handling PCI-sensitive card data, but can add friction compared to some simpler payment button integrations. Braintree is best suited for e-commerce businesses, software platforms and marketplaces that need to split or route payments to multiple parties, and subscription/SaaS businesses that need robust recurring billing — particularly those that want to offer PayPal, Venmo, and digital wallets alongside cards without managing multiple separate payment integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Braintree is a payment gateway founded in Chicago in 2007 by Bryan Johnson and acquired by PayPal in 2013 for roughly $800 million. It has operated as a PayPal service since, combining its own developer-focused platform with PayPal's broader payments infrastructure.

The platform's core pitch is a single API that lets merchants accept cards, PayPal, Venmo, and major digital wallets without integrating each payment method separately, making it popular with e-commerce businesses, marketplaces, and SaaS platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Braintree offers native SDKs for iOS and Android plus JavaScript SDKs for web, supporting both fully custom checkout flows and a pre-built Drop-in UI for faster implementation. A Vault stores tokenized payment methods for recurring billing and future purchases.

Fraud and risk management tools are built on PayPal's underlying fraud detection infrastructure, and the platform supports recurring billing and subscription management for SaaS and subscription-based businesses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Braintree's standard U.S. pricing is 2.9% of the transaction amount plus $0.30 per transaction, with a $15 chargeback fee per dispute. Established or high-volume merchants can negotiate custom flat-rate or interchange-plus pricing instead of the standard blended rate; Braintree processes over $50 billion annually across more than one billion transactions per quarter.

features.title: Single API Integration
features.description: One integration to accept cards, PayPal, Venmo, Apple Pay, Google Pay, and other digital wallets.

features.title: Mobile & Web SDKs
features.description: Native SDKs for iOS and Android plus JavaScript SDKs for web, including a pre-built Drop-in UI.

features.title: Recurring Billing
features.description: Built-in subscription and recurring billing management for SaaS and subscription businesses.

features.title: Vault (Tokenization)
features.description: Securely stores customer payment methods for card-on-file and future transactions.

features.title: Fraud & Risk Tools
features.description: Fraud detection and risk management built on PayPal's underlying infrastructure.

features.title: Split & Marketplace Payments
features.description: Supports routing and splitting payments across multiple parties for platform and marketplace use cases.

pros: Single integration for cards, PayPal, Venmo, and major digital wallets
Backed by PayPal's scale, infrastructure, and fraud protection
Native mobile SDKs for iOS and Android
High-volume merchants can negotiate custom rates
cons: Standard blended pricing isn't the cheapest option at high volume vs. negotiated interchange-plus
$15 chargeback fee per disputed transaction
Requires an approval/underwriting process before going live
Support responsiveness can vary depending on account size

faqs.question: Who owns Braintree?
faqs.answer: Braintree is owned by PayPal, which acquired the company in 2013 for approximately $800 million.

faqs.question: What are Braintree's standard fees?
faqs.answer: Standard U.S. pricing is 2.9% of the transaction amount plus $0.30 per transaction, with a $15 fee per chargeback; high-volume merchants can negotiate custom rates.

faqs.question: What payment methods does Braintree support?
faqs.answer: Braintree supports credit and debit cards, PayPal, Venmo, Apple Pay, Google Pay, and other digital wallets through a single API.

faqs.question: Does Braintree support mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, Braintree provides native SDKs for both iOS and Android, alongside JavaScript SDKs for web checkout.

faqs.question: How much payment volume does Braintree process?
faqs.answer: Braintree processes over $50 billion in payments annually and more than one billion transactions per quarter.

use_cases.title: E-commerce checkout
use_cases.description: Online retailers use Braintree to accept cards, PayPal, and digital wallets through one integrated checkout.

use_cases.title: Marketplace and platform payments
use_cases.description: Platforms and marketplaces use Braintree to split and route payments to multiple sellers or service providers.

use_cases.title: Subscription billing
use_cases.description: SaaS and subscription businesses use Braintree's recurring billing and Vault to manage stored payment methods and renewals.

enrichment.short_description: Brand24 is an AI-powered social listening and media monitoring tool that tracks brand mentions across social media, news, blogs, and forums in real time.
enrichment.seo_title: Brand24: AI Social Listening & Brand Monitoring Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Brand24 is an AI-powered social listening platform that tracks brand mentions across social media, news, and forums, with sentiment analysis and real-time alerts.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Brand24 is a social listening and media monitoring platform founded in 2011 by Michal Sadowski and headquartered in Wrocław, Poland. The company is publicly traded on the Warsaw Stock Exchange and is majority-owned by Prowly, itself a subsidiary of Semrush, the American SEO and marketing analytics company. Brand24's core function is to track online mentions of a brand, product, competitor, or topic across social media platforms, news sites, blogs, forums, podcasts, videos, and review sites, aggregating them into a single dashboard. The platform has increasingly emphasized AI-driven analysis, including AI sentiment analysis that automatically classifies mentions as positive, negative, or neutral; an AI Brand Assistant; AI Insights and AI Topics features that summarize themes and trends across large volumes of mentions; and AI Events Detection for spotting sudden spikes or anomalies in mention volume, such as a PR crisis or viral moment. Additional capabilities include influencer scoring to identify which authors are driving the most reach or engagement, share-of-voice and trend analysis for competitive benchmarking, and integrations with tools like Slack and Google Data Studio for reporting and alerting. Brand24 publishes its pricing transparently on its website, structured around five tiers. The Individual plan starts at $199/month billed annually (or $249/month billed monthly) and covers 3 keywords, roughly 2,000 mentions per month, 1 user, and updates every 12 hours. The Team plan is $299/month annually ($349 monthly), covering 7 keywords, 10,000 mentions/month, unlimited users, and hourly updates. The Pro plan is $399/month annually ($499 monthly), covering 12 keywords, 40,000 mentions/month, unlimited users, real-time updates, and the fuller AI feature set (AI Events Detection, AI Brand Assistant, AI Insights and AI Topics for a limited number of projects). The Business plan is $599/month annually ($699 monthly), covering 25 keywords, 100,000 mentions/month, and expanded AI Insights/Topics access plus a dedicated Client Success Lead. An Enterprise plan offers custom keyword and mention limits with unlimited AI Insights and Topics, dedicated consulting, and an AI Visibility module, with pricing starting around $1,499 and scaling by negotiated agreement. All paid plans are backed by a 14-day free trial that does not require a credit card. Brand24 is generally positioned as a more accessible, mid-market alternative to larger enterprise social listening suites like Brandwatch or Meltwater, making it popular with small and medium businesses, marketing agencies managing multiple client brands, and PR teams that need real-time crisis and reputation monitoring without an enterprise sales process.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Brand24 is a social listening and media monitoring platform founded in 2011 by Michal Sadowski, headquartered in Wrocław, Poland, and publicly traded on the Warsaw Stock Exchange under majority ownership tied to Semrush via its Prowly subsidiary.

The platform tracks brand, product, competitor, or topic mentions across social media, news, blogs, forums, podcasts, videos, and reviews, surfacing them in a unified dashboard with AI-driven sentiment and trend analysis.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Brand24's AI features include automated sentiment analysis, an AI Brand Assistant, AI Insights and AI Topics for summarizing themes across mentions, and AI Events Detection to flag sudden spikes in mention volume that may indicate a PR issue or viral moment.

Additional tools include influencer scoring to identify high-impact authors, share-of-voice and trend analysis for competitive benchmarking, and integrations with Slack and Google Data Studio for alerts and reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Brand24 publishes transparent pricing across five tiers: Individual ($199-249/month), Team ($299-349/month), Pro ($399-499/month), Business ($599-699/month), and a custom Enterprise plan starting around $1,499. All paid plans include a 14-day free trial with no credit card required, and pricing scales with keyword limits, monthly mention volume, and how frequently data updates (from every 12 hours up to real time).

features.title: Real-Time Mention Tracking
features.description: Monitors social media, news, blogs, forums, and review sites for brand, product, or competitor mentions.

features.title: AI Sentiment Analysis
features.description: Automatically classifies mentions as positive, negative, or neutral to gauge brand perception at scale.

features.title: AI Events Detection
features.description: Flags sudden spikes or anomalies in mention volume that may indicate a crisis or viral moment.

features.title: Influencer Scoring
features.description: Identifies which authors and accounts are driving the most reach and engagement around a topic.

features.title: AI Insights & Topics
features.description: Summarizes recurring themes and trends across large volumes of mentions automatically.

features.title: Reporting Integrations
features.description: Connects with Slack and Google Data Studio for alerts, dashboards, and custom reporting.

pros: Transparent, publicly listed pricing across all tiers
Strong AI sentiment and insight features relative to price point
14-day free trial with no credit card required
More accessible pricing than enterprise-only competitors like Brandwatch
cons: Mention volume caps on lower tiers can be limiting for larger brands
Real-time update frequency is reserved for Pro tier and above
Historical data access is more limited on entry-level plans
Costs scale quickly moving from Individual up to Business/Enterprise tiers

faqs.question: How much does Brand24 cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start at $199/month billed annually (Individual) and scale up through Team, Pro, Business, and a custom Enterprise tier starting around $1,499, based on keyword and mention limits.

faqs.question: Does Brand24 offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, all paid plans include a 14-day free trial that does not require a credit card.

faqs.question: Does Brand24 use AI?
faqs.answer: Yes. Brand24 includes AI sentiment analysis, an AI Brand Assistant, AI Insights and Topics for theme summarization, and AI Events Detection for spotting mention spikes.

faqs.question: Who owns Brand24?
faqs.answer: Brand24 is a publicly traded Polish company on the Warsaw Stock Exchange, majority-owned by Prowly, a subsidiary of Semrush.

faqs.question: How is Brand24 different from Brandwatch?
faqs.answer: Brand24 offers transparent, self-serve pricing aimed at SMBs and agencies, while Brandwatch is a larger enterprise platform with custom, sales-negotiated pricing.

use_cases.title: Brand reputation monitoring
use_cases.description: Marketing and PR teams track sentiment and volume around their brand to catch reputation issues early.

use_cases.title: Agency multi-client monitoring
use_cases.description: Marketing agencies monitor mentions across multiple client brands from a single platform.

use_cases.title: Campaign and competitor tracking
use_cases.description: Brands track share of voice and campaign buzz against competitors in real time.

features: AI Events Detection, AI Brand Assistant, AI Insights & Topics (limited projects)

enrichment.short_description: Brandwatch is an AI-powered consumer intelligence and social media management platform, owned by Cision, that analyzes conversations across 100M+ online sources.
enrichment.seo_title: Brandwatch: AI Consumer Intelligence & Social Listening
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Brandwatch is an enterprise consumer intelligence and social media management platform owned by Cision, analyzing 100M+ online sources for brand insights.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Brandwatch is an enterprise consumer intelligence and social media management platform founded by Giles Palmer, who incorporated the predecessor company Runtime Collective Ltd in Brighton, UK, in 1999/2005; the business relaunched its social monitoring product as Brandwatch in 2007, focusing on tracking social media mentions and measuring social advertising impact. The company remains headquartered in Brighton, United Kingdom, and has grown to more than 1,000 employees across roughly 15-17 offices worldwide, including New York, London, Copenhagen, Berlin, Sofia, Paris, Singapore, and Sydney. In February 2021, Brandwatch was acquired by Cision, the PR and media monitoring company, for $450 million in a combined cash-and-shares deal, combining Brandwatch's social listening and consumer intelligence capabilities with Cision's media monitoring and press/media contact database business. Brandwatch has continued to operate under its own brand post-acquisition rather than being folded into the Cision name. In May 2022, Falcon.io — a social media management platform Cision had acquired in January 2019 — was merged into Brandwatch, meaning social media publishing, engagement, and scheduling tools formerly sold under the Falcon.io brand now live within Brandwatch's Social Media Management product line. Brandwatch today sells two main product lines: Consumer Intelligence and Social (Social Media Management). Consumer Intelligence itself comprises Consumer Research (which mines social media and other online data from more than 100 million sources for market research and brand-perception analysis), Vizia (a data visualization add-on for displaying live social/brand metrics on screens, often used in newsrooms or marketing operations centers), and Reviews (a product review monitoring and analysis tool). The platform emphasizes AI-driven analytics for large-scale conversation and sentiment analysis, image/visual listening to detect logos and visual brand mentions in photos, and trend and topic detection across its massive data footprint. Brandwatch reports serving more than 7,500 clients and states it is trusted by roughly half of the Forbes 100. As is typical for platforms operating at this scale and data volume, Brandwatch does not publish self-serve pricing; access requires a custom quote from its sales team based on data volume, modules licensed, and number of seats, positioning it as a premium, enterprise-first option relative to lower-cost self-serve social listening tools. Brandwatch also offers a public developer API — the Brandwatch API, covering Consumer Research, Publish, Engage, Measure, Data Upload, and Analysis endpoints, documented at developers.brandwatch.com — that lets enterprise customers pull mention, sentiment, and trend data into their own systems and BI tooling, though usage is rate-limited (30 calls per 10 minutes per client by default). Brandwatch is best suited to large enterprises, market research teams, and PR/communications agencies that need very large-scale social and consumer data coverage, dedicated onboarding, and combined listening-plus-publishing capabilities, rather than smaller teams looking for simpler, self-serve pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Brandwatch traces back to Runtime Collective Ltd, incorporated by Giles Palmer in Brighton, UK, and relaunched as Brandwatch in 2007 to focus on social media monitoring. The company remains headquartered in Brighton and has grown to more than 1,000 employees across roughly 15-17 global offices.

Cision acquired Brandwatch for $450 million in February 2021, and in May 2022 merged in Falcon.io — a social media management platform Cision had separately acquired — bringing publishing and engagement tools into the Brandwatch product line alongside its original listening and consumer intelligence capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Brandwatch's Consumer Intelligence product mines more than 100 million online sources for market research and brand perception, and includes Vizia for live data visualization and Reviews for product review monitoring, alongside AI-driven sentiment, trend, and image/visual listening capabilities.

The former Falcon.io functionality now lives inside Brandwatch's Social Media Management product, combining listening with social publishing and engagement in one platform. A public Brandwatch API also lets enterprise customers pull mention, sentiment, and analysis data into their own systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Brandwatch does not publish self-serve pricing. Access to Consumer Intelligence and Social Media Management requires a custom quote based on data volume, modules licensed, and seat count, reflecting its positioning as a premium enterprise platform serving more than 7,500 clients including roughly half of the Forbes 100.

features.title: Consumer Research
features.description: Mines more than 100 million online sources for market research and brand-perception analysis.

features.title: AI Sentiment & Trend Analysis
features.description: AI-powered analysis of conversation sentiment, trending topics, and volume across massive data sets.

features.title: Image/Visual Listening
features.description: Detects logos and visual brand mentions within photos shared across social platforms.

features.title: Vizia Data Visualization
features.description: Displays live social and brand metrics on screens, commonly used in newsrooms or marketing ops centers.

features.title: Social Media Management
features.description: Combines listening with social publishing, scheduling, and engagement tools inherited from the Falcon.io merger.

features.title: Reviews Monitoring
features.description: Tracks and analyzes product reviews across online sources.

features.title: Brandwatch API
features.description: Public API covering Consumer Research, Publish, Engage, Measure, Data Upload, and Analysis endpoints for custom integrations.

pros: Massive data source coverage (100M+ online sources)
Combines social listening with publishing/engagement after the Falcon.io merger
Strong AI-driven sentiment, trend, and image/visual listening
Enterprise-grade support and onboarding backed by Cision
cons: No public pricing; requires a sales-negotiated enterprise contract
Expensive relative to SMB-focused social listening tools like Brand24
Steeper learning curve given the breadth of the platform
API usage is rate-limited by default (30 calls/10 minutes per client)

faqs.question: Who owns Brandwatch?
faqs.answer: Brandwatch is owned by Cision, which acquired it for $450 million in February 2021; Brandwatch continues to operate under its own brand.

faqs.question: How much does Brandwatch cost?
faqs.answer: Brandwatch does not publish pricing; access requires a custom quote from sales based on data volume, modules, and seats.

faqs.question: What happened to Falcon.io?
faqs.answer: Falcon.io, a social media management platform previously acquired by Cision, was merged into Brandwatch in May 2022, and its former features now live within Brandwatch's Social Media Management product.

faqs.question: Does Brandwatch have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Brandwatch API offers Consumer Research, Publish, Engage, Measure, Data Upload, and Analysis endpoints for enterprise integrations, documented at developers.brandwatch.com.

faqs.question: Where is Brandwatch headquartered?
faqs.answer: Brandwatch is headquartered in Brighton, United Kingdom, with additional offices worldwide including New York, London, Berlin, Paris, Singapore, and Sydney.
